fn1: The word prajapati means lord of the world and has different nuances. It is a name for Brahma. However, those who were appointed by Brahma to rule over the worlds are also known as Prajapati. Accordingly, Daksha is also known as Prajapati and the reference here is to Daksha.
fn2: The word used is yayavaras, an expression also used by Jaratkaru’s ancestors.
fn3: Vasuki is a great naga or serpent and is the king of the nagas. Vasuki was the rope when the ocean was churned by the gods and the demons.
fn4: Kadru and Vinata were daughters of Daksha and were married to the sage Kashyapa. Kadru was the mother of the snakes and as the story unfolds, we will learn that Kadru cursed her offspring because they did not side with her in her rivalry with Vinata.
fn10: The son. Aruna is the red sky of dawn and is also the sun’s charioteer.
fn13: Mythical beings with human bodies and heads of horses.
fn14: A yojana is a measure of distance and is equal to four kroshas. A yojana is between 13 and 15 kilometres.
fn15: Ananta literally means without end and is a name for the naga Shesha, on whom Vishnu rests, and who is Vasuki’s elder brother. Instructed by Vishnu or Narayana, Ananta uprooted Mandara.
fn17: In a loose sense, asuras and danavas are both demons. But asura is more of a general term and means the antithesis of suras or gods. Danavas are more speciﬁc demons, in the sense that they are the offspring of Danu. Daityas are also speciﬁc demons, in the sense that they are the offspring of Diti.
fn27: The heads were adorned with gold ornaments.
fn28: Weapons had to be summoned with the power of the mind.
fn32: Indra. Indra had killed a demon named Bala.
fn33: Someone wearing a crown or diadem. Kiriti is another name for Arjuna. In this context, it probably means the god Nara.
fn35: This should not cause confusion. The word Kourava is often used as opposite to Pandava. The Pandavas had Pandu as their father. However, Kuru was a common ancestor to both brothers. Hence any Pandava, and anyone descended from the Pandavas, can also be called Kourava.
fn38: The word used is timingila. A whale is a timi, a timingila is that which swallows whales.
fn39: The word used is makara. Since a makara is a mythical being, it is impossible to translate it satisfactorily. But shark or crocodile is close enough.
fn41: Vishnu. This is a reference to Vishnu’s boar (varaha) incarnation.
fn43: As will be evident, this really means the gods.
fn45: Another name for Garuda, meaning the one with beautiful feathers.
fn48: The word means destroyer of cities.
fn53: A kshana is a small interval of time, the time it takes for a twinkling of the eye and is equal to four-ﬁfths of a second. A lava is even smaller, because it is one-sixth of a kshana.
fn54: The bright half of the lunar month.
fn55: The dark half of the lunar month. The term used in the text is bahula.
fn56: The daily increase or decrease in the size of the moon. A kala is the span of a zodiac sign divided by 1800. So it is roughly around 1 minute, as in minutes and degrees. Measured in time, a kala is variously deﬁned as 1 minute, 48 seconds or 8 seconds.
fn58: Very small interval of time, equal to half of a lava.
fn61: Makara. However, a makara is actually a mythical being.
fn64: The Malaya mountains are in south India, adjoining Malabar. The Malaya mountains had several sandalwood trees. Thus, an alternative translation is that the island had many sandalwood trees, since malaya also means a sandalwood tree.
fn67: Amrita is nectar or ambrosia and was produced when the ocean was churned.
fn68: Hunters, ﬁshermen. Usually, they are described as dwelling in the mountains.
fn70: Supratika is a common name for an elephant and this elephant should not be confused with Bhagadatta’s elephant Supratika.
fn71: A yojana is between 13 and 15 kilometres.
fn75: Rishis who number 60,000 and were generated from the creator’s body. They are the size of a thumb and precede the sun’s chariot.
fn80: The performer of a hundred sacriﬁces, Indra.
fn81: Great sage and preceptor of the gods.
fn85: A tree with large, fragrant and orange-coloured ﬂowers, the Butea frondosa.
fn94: Aruna is the dawn and also the sun’s charioteer.
fn95: Vishvakarma is the architect of the gods and Bhouvana (or Bhoumana) is one of his names.
fn100: That is, enter the place where the amrita was.
fn105: Dhritarashtra is also the name of a naga and this should not be confused with the Dhritarashtra who was Duryodhana’s father.
fn108: This can also be translated as the forest of Pushkara.
fn114: These words are being addressed to Vasuki.
fn123: Vaivasvata is one of Yama’s names.
fn124: This refers to Shamika’s message, not the curse. The curse was levied by Shamika’s son, Shringi.
fn127: The name used in the text is Nagasahnya.
fn129: Yudhishthira, who in a sense was also the great-grandfather, though not as directly as Arjuna.
fn131: An ancient king of the lunar dynasty and therefore an ancestor of the Kouravas and the Pandavas. Pururava married the apsara Urvashi.
fn132: That is, the merit obtained through these austerities.
fn133: A brahmachari, usually translated as celibate. However, a brahmachari is strictly someone who treads the path of the brahman.
fn135: Shvetakakiya. This is an unhappy translation, though white crow is right. However, the sense is that the dedication has the watchfulness of a dog, the timidity of a deer and the instinct of a crow.
fn137: Agnihotra is a sacriﬁcial ﬁre. The text means that the time for making offerings to the sacriﬁcial ﬁre has arrived.
fn138: Asti can be translated as ‘it is there’. Jaratkaru meant that an embryo was there in the womb.
fn139: Shiva, the one with a trident in his hand.
fn140: Dhanur Veda. This is about the science of ﬁghting and weaponry.
fn142: Soubhadra means Subhadra’s son, that is, Abhimanyu. Parikshit was Abhimanyu’s son. Parikshina means to become weak, thin, lean, emaciated. Since this is what had happened to the Kuru lineage, he came to be known as Parikshit.
fn143: Kama (desire), krodha (anger), lobha (greed), mada (ego), moha (delusion) and matsarya (envy).
fn145: Takshaka had assumed the disguise of a Brahmana.
fn146: This is now a reference to Kashyapa.
fn148: Superintending or assisting priests; probably means assisting here.
fn149: Ofﬁciating priests. Usually, there were four types of ritvijas—hotar (one who recited prayers, identiﬁed with Rig Veda), udgatar (one who chanted or sang prayers, identiﬁed with Sama Veda), adhvaryu (one who ofﬁciated, identiﬁed with Yajur Veda) and brahman (chief priest, identiﬁed with Atharva Veda).
fn153: Send the sacriﬁce up to the gods.
fn159: The text should literally be translated as the staff of a Brahmana.
fn164: The ﬁre. Vibhavasu is also a name for the sun.
fn165: The ﬁre. Chitrabhanu is also a name for the sun.
